companyCaseStudy.title: How Discord Scaled Real-Time Voice & Chat for 140M Users: Elixir, WebRTC, and Millions of Connections
companyCaseStudy.summary: Discord grew to 140M+ monthly users and had to support millions of concurrent real-time connections (text + voice) with low latency and low cost. They rethought the gateway, leveraged BEAM/Elixir for connection management, and built a custom voice stack (WebRTC + SFUs), cutting CPU and operational complexity while keeping tail latency under tight bounds.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook — millions of sockets, tiny tails

Discord reported well over 100M monthly active users as it exploded from gamer chat to a general-purpose real-time communication platform. That meant: millions of simultaneous WebSocket connections, spikes when major communities rallied, and voice/video sessions that must not stutter — all while keeping costs sane. The core challenge: how do you keep millions of long-lived TCP/TLS connections, route real-time messages and voice packets with sub-200ms tails, and survive join storms and global events?

---

## Problem → Constraints

- Scale: 140M+ MAU, with "millions" of concurrent connections across regions.
- Real-time: sub-200ms chat delivery and <50ms audio jitter targets for good UX.
- Long-lived connections: millions of idle-but-alive sockets consume file descriptors, memory, and scheduling.
- Cost & ops: avoid linear cost growth; minimize complexity for ops and deploys.

### Why classic stacks failed

- Ruby/Puma or Node single-threaded processes struggled to hold massive numbers of sockets efficiently and to provide predictable latency under head-of-line blocking.
- Sharding WebSocket connections across many heavy processes increased operational overhead and the blast radius for failures.

---

## The Architectural Choices (high level)

1. Move the connection gateway to BEAM (Erlang VM) / Elixir for massive concurrent, lightweight processes.
2. Use a small set of services for routing presence & messages; push heavier work (storage, ranking, analytics) out to backend services.
3. Build voice on top of WebRTC with custom SFUs (Selective Forwarding Units) to avoid sending media via a slow central server for every peer.
4. Use Redis and sharded state for presence and routing, plus Kafka-like pipelines for async durability and analytics.

### Why the BEAM (Elixir/Erlang)?

- Lightweight processes: millions of green threads each holding a socket (far lighter than OS threads).
- Preemptive scheduling: one noisy connection can't starve others, improving tail latency.
- Built-in supervision trees and hot code upgrades reduced MTTR and kept services resilient under churn.

---

## Solution — Components & Flow

### Core components

- Gateway (Elixir): accepts WebSocket/TCP/TLS, manages heartbeats and per-connection state.
- Presence Layer (Redis / sharded stores): tracks which gateway node owns which connection, used for routing messages and presence queries.
- Routing Service (Elixir/Go): maps an in-room event to N gateway node target(s).
- Voice SFUs (C++/Go): relay RTP/DTLS/SRTP streams selectively between participants, with region-aware placement.
- Async pipelines (Kafka / Pulsar / internal queue): fan-out for analytics, moderation, message persistence.

### Flow: A text message fan-out

mermaid
flowchart TD
  ClientA[Client (WebSocket)] -->|Send msg| Gateway1[Gateway (Elixir)]
  Gateway1 --> Router[Routing Service]
  Router -->|Look up members| Presence[Presence Store (Redis)]
  Presence --> Router
  Router -->|Push to gateway nodes| Gateway2[Gateway (Elixir)]
  Gateway2 --> ClientB[Client (WebSocket)]
  Gateway2 -->|Emit to async| Pipeline[(Kafka/Pulsar)]

This keeps the hot-path lightweight: Gateway handles sockets + heartbeats; Router + Presence do quick fan-out decisions; heavy work like persistence and analytics are async.

### Voice flow (simplified)

mermaid
graph LR
  Caller --> GW[Gateway]
  Caller --> SFU1[SFU (Regional)]
  Callee --> GW2[Gateway]
  Callee --> SFU1
  SFU1 --> SFU2[Cross-region SFU]:::cross
  classDef cross stroke:#f66;

- Clients establish WebRTC DTLS/SRTP with the SFU(s).
- SFUs forward selected tracks (audio/video) based on room topology and bandwidth.
- Gateways handle control signaling, not the RTP plane.

---

## Concrete results & numbers

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------:|------:|
| Long-lived connections per node | ~tens of thousands (heavy OS threads) | hundreds of thousands (BEAM processes) |
| Tail latency (99.9%) for message delivery | 800ms+ during spikes | <200ms for typical spikes |
| Voice jitter/packet-loss during peak events | noticeable user complaints | sub-50ms jitter targets met in-region |
| Operational incidents related to gateway crashes | frequent cascading failures | far fewer; smaller blast radius via supervision trees |

Operationally, Discord reported that moving to BEAM-based gateways allowed them to host far more connections per machine and reduce operational complexity of connection management. Using SFUs for voice kept media costs from multiplying with every participant, reducing bandwidth and CPU usage on the core by large factors during big calls.

---

## Trade-offs and gotchas

- BEAM is great for many lightweight processes, but heavy CPU work (media processing, ML inference) still belongs in native processes or GPUs.
- Presence stores become a scaling bottleneck — Discord shards presence and uses region locality aggressively.
- SFU topology matters: poorly placed SFUs increase cross-region bandwidth and hurt latency.

---

## Why this worked (engineering principles)

- Right tool for the job: BEAM for connection density and predictable scheduling; native services for media processing.
- Separate control plane (signaling/presence) from data plane (RTP media streams) to scale independently.
- Asynchronous durability and fan-out (message persistence to queues) to keep the user path fast.

---

## Incident & reliability patterns

- Supervision trees and circuit breakers on gateway nodes limited failover blast radius.
- Heartbeat and lease expiry in presence stores made ghost connections rare and sped recovery.
- Chaos testing simulated join storms to validate gateway and SFU autoscaling policies.

---

## Final numbers and business impact

- Supported 140M+ MAU with multi-million concurrent connections patterns.
- Reduced gateway-related incidents and dropped connections by a large margin (publicly described as “significant operational improvement” by Discord engineers).
- Cost-effective scaling: fewer larger, more efficient machines rather than many small brittle processes.

---

## TL;DR

Discord reached scale by using BEAM/Elixir for connection density and predictability, moving media to regionally-placed SFUs, sharding presence, and keeping the user path tiny and async-heavy work out of the hot path. The result: millions of connections, lower tails, and fewer operational headaches.
keyTakeaways: Use the right runtime: BEAM (Elixir/Erlang) supports millions of lightweight processes ideal for long-lived socket connections — dramatically improving tail latency and density.
Separate control and data planes: keep signaling and presence lightweight; route media via SFUs to avoid N×N media costs.
Design for regional locality and shard presence/state early — global single-source presence becomes a bottleneck at millions of connections.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
defmodule Gateway.Connection do
  use GenServer

  # Each connection is a lightweight process — BEAM can run millions
  def start_link(socket) do
    GenServer.start_link(__MODULE__, socket)
  end

  def init(socket) do
    :ok = :ssl.setopts(socket, [{:active, true}])
    schedule_heartbeat()
    {:ok, %{socket: socket}}
  end

  def handle_info(:heartbeat, state) do
    # respond to heartbeat, check liveness
    send_heartbeat(state.socket)
    schedule_heartbeat()
    {:noreply, state}
  end

  defp schedule_heartbeat(), do: Process.send_after(self(), :heartbeat, 30_000)
  defp send_heartbeat(socket), do: :ssl.send(socket, "ping")

concept.title: From Ledgers to Lightning Reads: Event Sourcing + CQRS Explained
concept.summary: Event Sourcing plus CQRS turns every state change into an immutable history and separates the write model from the read model. This combo solves auditing, complex business logic, and scale for read-heavy systems — but it comes with modeling and operational trade-offs.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters

Many systems start as simple CRUD apps but quickly run into problems: auditability requirements, complex reports, temporal queries (what was the state yesterday?), and slow joins on heavy read paths. *Event Sourcing + CQRS* addresses those problems by changing what you persist (events) and how you serve reads (materialized projections).

**Analogy:** Instead of keeping a single ever-changing spreadsheet row for a bank account, you keep a ledger of every deposit/withdrawal. The balance is a projection (sum) of the ledger. The ledger is the *source of truth*.

### High-level idea

- **Event Sourcing:** Persist domain events (what happened) as an append-only log. The log is the canonical state. The current state is a projection (replay of events).
- **CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation):** Split the write path (commands -> events) from the read path (projections -> queries). Each can be optimized independently.

### Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>CommandHandler: PlaceOrder(command)
  CommandHandler->>EventStore: Append(OrderPlacedEvent)
  EventStore-->>CommandHandler: Ack
  Note right of EventStore: Event stream persisted (append-only)
  EventStore->>ProjectionService: Publish(Event)
  ProjectionService-->>ReadModel: Update materialized view
  Client->>QueryAPI: GetOrderDetails(query)
  QueryAPI->>ReadModel: Read (fast)
  ReadModel-->>QueryAPI: Data
  QueryAPI-->>Client: Response
```

### Flowchart (simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Command] --> B{Validation}
  B -->|Valid| C[Append Event to Store]
  C --> D[Publish to Event Bus]
  D --> E[Projections / Read Models]
  E --> F[Query API]
  B -->|Invalid| G[Reject]
```

### Before / After (CRUD vs Event Sourcing + CQRS)

| Before (CRUD) | After (Event Sourcing + CQRS) |
|---------------|-------------------------------|
| Update in-place, overwriting previous state | Append immutable events; full history retained |
| Hard to reconstruct past or audit changes | Every change is auditable and replayable |
| Single model for reads and writes | Separate optimized read models (fast queries) |
| Migrations often require data transformations | Schema changes handled by replaying events to new projections |
| Simpler initially | More upfront complexity; powerful long-term benefits |

### When to use it

- Systems that require strong audit trails (financial, billing, regulatory).
- Apps with many and diverse read models (dashboards, reporting, mobile views).
- When you need temporal queries (state at time T), debugging via replay, or event-driven integrations.
- Avoid for simple CRUD apps or teams without bandwidth for the extra complexity and operations.

### Practical example (Node.js - simplified)

```javascript
// very small in-memory event store + projection
const store = {};

function appendEvent(streamId, event) {
  store[streamId] = store[streamId] || [];
  store[streamId].push({...event, timestamp: Date.now()});
}

// projection: reconstruct current state
function projectAccount(streamId) {
  const events = store[streamId] || [];
  return events.reduce((acc, e) => {
    if (e.type === 'Deposit') acc.balance += e.amount;
    if (e.type === 'Withdraw') acc.balance -= e.amount;
    return acc;
  }, {balance: 0});
}

// usage
appendEvent('acct-1', {type: 'Deposit', amount: 100});
appendEvent('acct-1', {type: 'Withdraw', amount: 30});
console.log(projectAccount('acct-1')); // { balance: 70 }
```

### Common pitfalls

- **Treating events as a storage optimization.** Event Sourcing is a modeling change: events are the truth, not a noisy replication detail.
- **No event schema/version strategy.** If you don't plan for event versioning and migrations, replaying old events becomes painful.
- **Ignoring idempotency and concurrency.** Use optimistic concurrency (expectedVersion) or other strategies to avoid conflicting appends.
- **Over-splitting with CQRS.** Splitting read/write paths adds complexity; do it when you need separate scaling or read models.
- **Not operationalizing replay.** Replaying streams to rebuild projections must be tested and automated.

### Comparison Table

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| CRUD (state-in-place) | Simple, easy to reason about | No history, hard to audit, migrations risky | Small apps, low audit needs |
| Event Sourcing + CQRS | Full audit trail, replayability, flexible read models, good for complex domains | More complex, operational overhead, event versioning | Financials, analytics-heavy, complex business logic |

### How to migrate (practical path)
1. Start writing events for new features while keeping the existing CRUD model (dual-write).
2. Build projections for new read needs; gradually switch read traffic to projections.
3. Backfill event streams from historical state where possible, or accept hybrid state during migration.
4. Add robust tests for replay, idempotency, and concurrency; use feature flags to flip reads when safe.
keyTakeaways: Store facts (events) not just current state — events give you auditability, replay, and temporal queries.
Use CQRS to separate and optimize reads from writes; projections make queries fast and flexible.
Plan for schema/versioning, concurrency, and operational replay — the modeling benefits require engineering discipline.
